title: Two-gap to Single-gap Transition and Two-dome-like Superconductivity in Alkali-Metal Intercalated Bilayer PdTe2
authors: Yu-Lin Han, Shu-Xiang Qiao, Kai-Yue Jiang, Jie Zhang, Bao-Tian Wang, Ping Zhang, C. S. Ting, Hong-Yan Lu
abstract: PdTe2 has been synthesized with controllable thickness down to the monolayer limit. Based on first-principles calculations within the fully anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg framework, this work reveals that alkali-metal intercalation markedly enhances the weak superconductivity of bilayer PdTe2, boosting the transition temperature from 1.4 K to 5.0 -13.5 K and yielding a two-dome-like evolution of Tc. Rubidium intercalation induces the highest Tc of 13.5 K, which can be further increased to 14.5 K under biaxial tensile strain. The strain-dependent evolution of Tc also exhibits a two-dome-like behavior, reflecting the interplay between strain-induced band structure modifications and electron-phonon coupling (EPC). Moreover, a systematic correlation is identified between interlayer interaction and superconducting gap. Lithium intercalation induces a distinct two-gap state, whereas intercalants with larger atomic radii (Na, K, Rb, and Cs) drive the system into a single-gap character. The two-gap to single-gap transition originates from the modulation of interlayer coupling through intercalation-induced interlayer expansion. In addition, pristine and Li/Na-intercalated bilayers exhibit nontrivial band topology, suggesting that layered PdTe2 provides a promising platform for realizing the coexistence of superconductivity and nontrivial topology. These results provide detailed anisotropic insights into EPC and offer viable pathways for enhancing Tc and achieving diverse properties in layered PdTe2 systems.
summary: This study demonstrates that intercalating alkali metals into bilayer PdTe2 can boost its superconducting transition temperature by nearly an order of magnitude. The researchers show that the type of intercalant controls whether the system exhibits single-gap or two-gap superconductivity by modulating interlayer coupling. Furthermore, the work identifies a way to further enhance Tc using biaxial strain and highlights the potential for hosting nontrivial topology alongside superconductivity. These findings provide a roadmap for tuning the electronic and superconducting properties of layered transition-metal dichalcogenides.
main_problem: Investigating how alkali-metal intercalation and biaxial strain modulate the superconducting transition temperature, the transition from two-gap to single-gap superconductivity, and the coexistence of superconductivity with nontrivial band topology in bilayer PdTe2.
main_result: Alkali-metal intercalation significantly enhances Tc from 1-4 K to up to 13.5 K, exhibiting a two-dome-like evolution, while larger intercalants drive a transition from two-gap to single-gap superconductivity via interlayer expansion. Additionally, Rb-intercalated PdTe2 under tensile strain reaches 14.5 K and shows a two-dome-like Tc dependence.
method: First-principles calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT) with the PBE functional, combined with the fully anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg framework and Wannier interpolation.
model_or_system: The physical system is bilayer PdTe2 (a transition-metal dichalcogenide) in various states: pristine, alkali-metal intercalated (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs), and under biaxial tensile strain.
key_observables: Superconducting transition temperature (Tc), superconducting gap (delta), electron-phonon coupling constant (lambda), Eliashberg spectral function (alpha2F), and Z2 topological invariant.
important_parameters: Interlayer spacing (d), electron-phonon coupling (EPC), Fermi level (Ef), and biaxial strain (epsilon).
main_assumptions: Use of the PBE functional (which may slightly overestimate lattice constants) and the use of a typical Coulomb pseudopotential (mu*c = 0.14).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the unit cell of Pd2Te4, orbital-resolved electronic band structures, and Density of States (DOS).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the system, details the computational methods, analyzes the electronic and phonon properties of pristine and intercalated bilayers, explores the transition between two-gap and single-gap regimes, investigates strain effects on Tc, and concludes with the topological implications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Side and top views of Pd2Te4. The dark blue and light brown spheres represent Pd and Te atoms, respectively. The 2D hexagonal unit cell is indicated by the black rhombus. (b) Orbital-resolved electronic band structure and (c) density of states (DOS). (d) 2D maps of momentum-resolved EPC strength (λnk) on the FS. (e) Normalized distribution of EPC strength ρ(λnk). (f) Phonon dispersion weighted by the λqν. (g) Projected phonon DOS (PhDOS). (h) Eliashberg spec- tral function α2F(ω) and accumulated EPC constant λ(ω) of Pd2Te4.

FIG. 2. (a) Crystal structure of alkali-metal intercalated Pd2Te4, in which alkali-metal is represented by the deep pink spheres. (b) Formation energies as a function of alkali-metal concentration (x), take Li intercalation for example. The dash line illustrates the convex hull between Pd2Te4 (n = 0) and the body-centered cubic Li crystal (n = 1).

(f) Energy distribution of the anisotropic superconducting energy gap ∆nk as a func- tion of temperature, and the dashed lines serve as guides to the eye.
